The original lead generation ad was about like everybody else in their market. It was like a fourth of a page ad show, picture of a tarpon and a truck, a guy put a tarpon, call for free brochure, and so on and so forth. We eliminated that whole ad and changed it to the very front end. And then they would send out a brochure and then they'd go to the phone. Well, the ad got changed to a print type ad, you know, a warning type ad, before you buy any tarpon system, you know, very copy-intensive ad. No coupon, you would almost have to read the ad to even know how to respond. Then, 16-page sales letter and a request form for the video. They had either mail or fax or call now to get the video. The company has shifted from 80% of the sales being made by outbound telemarketing with 7 or more calls to the same prospect before closing a deal to 80% of the sales made by the prospect calling, by the decision-maker calling in, asking five or less questions and making a purchase in the same phone call. What's the use of this? $1,600, $1,700 per system in the average customer by 10 to 20 packages because they got fleets and they're not buying the first single drop. Yeah. But think of what's happening. First of all, the lead generation has changed so we get the decision-maker in the first place. We have a more interesting decision-maker because he had to read all that copy to respond. Now he gets a 16-page sales letter that he's got to read through. He can't read 16 pages or so. Not necessarily. He can scan it if he wants to and we'll talk about that a little bit later. But my sense of the customer is that most of them are reading every word. Yeah. I can tell you like in the U.S. Gold case, the package that goes out to them is a 16-page sales letter. And we handle all those calls internally at the company so that there's a place where I can get real data because I got five women and eight people, bribing all the asks and questions today and getting some results instead of like dealing with matrix. And those customers will refer to things throughout the letter or ask questions that clearly indicate they have read that entire piece. And these are blue collar Kmart Shoppers and Mama Coppers, this is Al Bundy by. And yet they've read 16 pages because they'll say something that they would only know if they had read page 18. So yeah, I believe the interested person reads it. And so we've inverted that company's entire marketing process. Their salespeople have gotten those how much more productive because they're not playing chase the decision maker on the phone anymore. They're on the phone with the decision maker. Next point, which you probably know, but that is forget everything you were ever taught about good for anyone. This stuff should totally be right like you talk. Yeah. I mean, it should be like a few weeks. Paul stuff goes up the certified financial partners. That's like he and a couple of those guys are sitting around him to beer at the end of the day in a bitch session. That's exactly what it is. And it sounds like that. Yeah. Why is there some kind of like the tone of some of the words that he used in it? Well, I think the people that respond to that like that. Oh, absolutely. Well, and then in fact, you know, we use that there's some. Like this. Yeah. Underwear kid. Yeah, we talked about how Peggy, how Peggy hates the title of the book. By the way, Peggy hates the title I put on this book. She says that it's bad enough that I do sit around a house in my underwear. Why tell anybody about it? I embarrass her. I'm a little unpolished when I do talk with a customer on the phone. I may be laying in a lounge chair in my shorts using a portable phone. I've even taken one call sitting on the commode. Well, I guess I just want you to understand I'm just a plain ordinary guy. A long suffering cubs fan. A thrilled three Pete DeBulls fan. I don't golf by bowl. I don't drink wine. I'm a Bud fan. That's right. That's right. People that are really dead. Oh, yeah. Well, this is for this is for me. This is for you. Emotion versus logic. Emotion versus logic. They call it emotional logic and selling. There always must be a logical justification for their action. But no matter who you are writing to, the most analytical guy in the world, understand that some portion in most cases, the majority of his action is going to be taken based on emotion. I did a campaign for a client about a year ago. Company sells fairly sophisticated sales training systems to very large companies. They got to have a sales force of maybe 500 people in the field or more to buy. So they're pretty good size companies. And he's either selling to the CEO or he's selling to the guy who runs the sales people and reports to the CEO, one level down a vice president of sales, a national sales manager, whatever. And this guy's entire pitch was, I mean, he had documentation of the yin and yang of, you know, the fours and aifters of this company. These numbers went up and this went up and these statistics, I mean, prove, you know, a perfect analytical argument. And he wasn't getting very good results. And there was not an ounce of emotion to that guy. All the benefits were corporate and the sales people productivity, the sales results, but nothing to that guy. And you got to talk to that guy, that national sales manager or that CEO, you know, and the ulcer he's getting, because all the guys in the Chicago territory have their heads up their ass and can't produce. And the headache he gets every Wednesday when the guy in charge of the Midwest comes in his office and pitches and moans and whines. And you got to talk to him and his stomach and his head and what he's like when he goes home on a Friday and can't relax and plays a shitty golf game on Saturday because his mind's on all the problems and how did he have with the sales force. That is his that's the missing part here. And that missing part made, you know, multiple percentage differences in his response. And so you can't do a devoid of emotion and you've got to get to the emotional to the personal level of whoever is going to make the decision. They also got to have the logic to support what they did. Pain, pleasure, that's the very simple basis of all NLP and it does work. You move people from being in pain to a pleasurable alternative. And it's a big mistake to try and do one without first doing the other. If I was selling a cruise to Hawaii, my pitch would begin in the pain category, not pleasure category. It would begin with the horrible winter we've all had in the east. How bad the weather's been. Slogan to work every day through the slush of the crap of the snow, you know, and all the stress and the tension of the trains not running in the cabs, not being on the streets. And I would create that massive feeling of angst before I moved into the beautiful cruise to Hawaii and the winds and the islands and the beautiful girls and the nightlife and all that. And the entire travel industry generally misses that. They start with product and they start in the pleasure category. You should come to Disney World because it's the happiest place on earth. But imagine the pitch you could build against the unhappiest place on earth. Girl, I'm where you right now. And people are much more responsive to escaping pain than they are to gaining pleasure. I like starting here selling proposition with the universal problem. Yeah, it relates to that. The guys are friends now, but the marriage counselor and my wife went to, that she and I went to for a little while. He said a very profound thing. He said, you know, nobody ever comes here to get to have a happier. They only come here when it is coming apart at the seams. Sure, what drives them is the pain and fear of loss, which is the next thing we're going to talk about, not the potential for gain. The guys who sell the better sex videos, you see their direct response ads now in a lot of magazines, and Playboy's infomercial, which works. Now those ads work, but personally I think I can do an ad infinitely better than those ads. But by exactly by aiming at those people who are having real problems and focusing on the pain and one paragraph on these tapes and how great they are and how good, but I think they miss the boat by not focusing on all the pain aspects of having a real bad sex life. Then what they do, which is just start with, here's how to make it better. How they're struggling with their bills and all this other stuff. Absolutely. Rather than trying to say, focusing on... Yes, see, otherwise you presume, and by the way, that's why you need better ads, see, because format restricts you in what you can do. See, again, if we're going to look at this. I have to borrow your phone. I have to tell them that you can do it. Certainly one of the things we know about the people who read these magazines, they answer a lot of ads. We know that. So what do we got here? I'm a self-confessed opportunity junkie for years. I bought books, taped kids opportunities, distributed ships and planes. Why? Because I had a job I hated that I'm gonna never done. Most of what I sent away for was useless, silly, and solid, too complicated, difficult. Say, we're into shared pain. Shared pain. We tend to be with the process. Yeah, and getting them into the frame of mind of being frustrated and in agony and unhappy, so that they are then ready for the positive solution. It's why selling security systems to people who have never been burgled or assaulted is inherently the most difficult thing on earth to do. Because it's very difficult to get somebody to have that emotional experience until they have that experience. Now, you can sell a security system to anybody the minute after they've had their house burgled. I mean, that's the way down of the century. But that particular emotional experience is very difficult to duplicate any other way. Because it is just such a unique and intense emotional experience, particularly for women, more so for women than men. But having the house burgled is for them like they physically rate. But to tell them that it's very hard to duplicate, it's very hard to create that pain. You can't even imagine that. But when that pain happens, boy, is it a driving force. Is that right on the TV prevention system? Never works. It's such a bitch. Is that the reason why? Yeah, it's real hard to create the pain. I mean, about the only hope you have is doing it through testimonials to people who tell what it was like. Nobody's ever made a work. Nobody's ever made a work. I mean, I don't think I do a lot for their house or whatever it is. But is that because you don't get enough people who have that problem? Yeah, so you're so scattered. Oh, you're so what? Yeah, so you're so scattered. See, boy, if you could get a list, you know, I mean, on Monday, the bad old days of selling security systems in the home, you know, with sales guys, there's a company, they, you know, they got consumer fraud judgments against them in many, many states. But their technique was because people have to feel it. They would go out two weeks before they worked in the neighborhood. They would go out and they would leave empty liquor bottles and early magazines and cigars and the bushes around all the houses and let people find this stuff. And then after about two weeks of this, they'd hit the neighborhood. Well, then it's hard to imagine. Yeah, they work, right? Oh, it works like a charm. You know, works like there's no tomorrow. But it's very hard to duplicate that immediately. You know, and so prevention has always been a very, very tough thing. For several years, I was a partner in a business that sold, they're now very common in the environment, they sell it at 7-Eleven, but the chemical deterrent key ring, you know, it has a little canister, a base or whatever. And we sold them in seminars, mostly to real estate salespeople, because you got 70, 80% women, and they're going to be at open houses and stuff or taking somebody out to see a house alone. And we had instructors, mostly women, who would go into the real estate office, get 20 or 30 of them together, and do a three-hour scare the shit out of them, you know, seminar, and then sell these, these counterfeiters. Our sales would, as much as five times per head, anytime there was in the news in the market area a rapist on the loose. I'll never forget the second year that I was involved in the business, we had in Phoenix, what was dubbed the real estate rapist. Because what this guy would do is make appointments with real estate women to come out and show an empty house, and then he would rape them. When this hit the news, I'm telling you, we made money like there was no tomorrow. I mean, we were sitting there every day just hoping they had caught the guy during the previous night, because when the news, those classes, that day, the money was just going to roll in, you know, because it was just on the news constantly, and they'd interview on the TV news these women that had just been raped, you know, but trying to duplicate that from scratch is very, very difficult. It's the hardest part of the category I've ever worked in. It's very hard. But in any case, what you've got to do is you've got to create that pain, because people will do all sorts of things to escape pain, that they will not do to get positive things, and they will do it to escape it, not to prevent it. There's, on your next page, the handful of folks with the evidence. Yeah, before we go to that fear of loss, desire for gain, this is something that I have to deal with all the time. One of my divisions, we have 50 salespeople, and we're telemarketers for one specific product. And we always have a discussion about this. If I give them bonuses to achieve certain amount of sales, does it work? Or if I say, I'm going to take something away, do you do the work there? I don't know. It seems to me, I will bonus my salesman, where it doesn't matter. Salesman, I've been selling all my life, it seems that people will go to a certain level of comfort, and they'll stay there. I can show them how to make another $10,000 a month. They don't want to hear about it. They'd rather be selling it to the boss. Yeah, it's a side issue. It doesn't necessarily have to do with how this applies to copy. But your specific question, with sales forces? No, I'm looking at copy now. I know my salesman. Each different situation, if the bonus is to take something away depending on what it is. But as a sales letter or presentation on TV, I don't know if the desire for gain is going to make them do more than a pure profit. They say, okay, then, if I tell you how to make $10,000 by doing this, I don't know if you're going to do it. If you don't do this in this letter, and right now the IRS is going to take an additional $10,000, I know you're going to do that. Absolutely. You're absolutely right. Let's just briefly digress and take your first issue. The reason why it gets messed up when you're dealing with live sales people is because there's another major factor in life that controls their performance. And that is their internal self-image of what they're supposed to make. And whether you bonus or penalize, a person will not deviate dramatically from the range that they have internally set up of how much money they're supposed to make. Some people have that thoroughly ingrained from childhood, and until you undo it, unless something accidentally happens to change that person's belief system. We had a guy working for us. He's the most profound example that I've ever seen. He was with me for five years. And this guy, if he made $4,000 in commissions by the third day of the month, you would see for the next 27 days, the most incredible collection of calamities happen to an individual that could ever happen to anybody. The guy's car would be stolen, his house would burn down, his mother-in-law would fall down the steps of Toledo. He would get a disease. Everything to prevent him from making another dollar. If he had a tough month, on the 28th day, he would grind out the last deal to make $4,000 that month. This is the end of side one. Please turn the cassette over for side two.
